meta.description: FODMAP food guide for Crohn's disease dietary management
meta.disclaimer: Individual tolerance varies. Reintroduce foods one at a time under dietitian guidance.

high.name: Apple
high.notes: High in fructose and sorbitol. Cooked may be slightly better tolerated.

high.name: Pear
high.notes: Very high in excess fructose. Avoid during elimination.

high.name: Mango
high.notes: High fructose content especially when very ripe.

high.name: Watermelon
high.notes: Contains excess fructose and mannitol.

high.name: Cherry
high.notes: Contains sorbitol. Small servings (3-4) may be tolerated.

high.name: Peach
high.notes: High in sorbitol. Canned in syrup is also high.

high.name: Plum
high.notes: Contains sorbitol. Prunes are very high FODMAP.

high.name: Apricot
high.notes: High sorbitol content, especially dried apricots.

high.name: Nectarine
high.notes: Contains sorbitol similar to peach.

high.name: Blackberry
high.notes: High in excess fructose and polyols.

high.name: Dried fruit (dates, figs)
high.notes: Concentrated fructose. Very high FODMAP.

high.name: Garlic
high.notes: One of the highest fructan sources. Use garlic-infused oil instead.

high.name: Onion
high.notes: High in fructans in all forms (raw, cooked, powder). Green part of spring onion is low.

high.name: Leek (white part)
high.notes: White and light green parts are high. Dark green part is low.

high.name: Shallot
high.notes: Similar fructan content to onion.

high.name: Artichoke
high.notes: Very high in fructans and inulin.

high.name: Asparagus
high.notes: High fructan content. Tips may be lower than stalks.

high.name: Beetroot
high.notes: High in fructans and GOS. Small canned portions may be tolerated.

high.name: Brussels sprout
high.notes: Contains fructans and GOS. Often causes gas.

high.name: Cauliflower
high.notes: Contains mannitol. Small servings may be tolerated.

high.name: Mushroom
high.notes: Most varieties high in mannitol. Oyster mushrooms are lower.

high.name: Snow peas
high.notes: High in GOS (galacto-oligosaccharides).

high.name: Sugar snap peas
high.notes: Contains both fructans and GOS.

high.name: Savoy cabbage
high.notes: Higher FODMAP than regular cabbage. Limit servings.

high.name: Celery (large serve)
high.notes: Low in small serves but high mannitol in larger amounts.

high.name: Sweet corn
high.notes: Contains sorbitol and fructans in larger servings.

high.name: Wheat bread
high.notes: Wheat is a major fructan source. Sourdough spelt may be better tolerated.

high.name: Wheat pasta
high.notes: Use rice, quinoa, or gluten-free pasta instead.

high.name: Rye bread
high.notes: High in fructans. Sourdough rye may be slightly lower.

high.name: Barley
high.notes: Contains fructans. Avoid in soups and stews.

high.name: Couscous
high.notes: Made from wheat semolina, high in fructans.

high.name: Gnocchi
high.notes: Wheat-based. Gluten-free versions may be lower.

high.name: Granola with wheat/honey
high.notes: Double FODMAP hit from wheat fructans and honey fructose.

high.name: Cow's milk
high.notes: Lactose-free milk is a suitable substitute.

high.name: Yogurt (regular)
high.notes: Greek yogurt strained of whey is lower. Lactose-free varieties available.

high.name: Ice cream
high.notes: High lactose plus often high fructose from syrups.

high.name: Soft cheese (ricotta, cottage)
high.notes: Higher lactose than aged cheeses. Small serves of cottage cheese may be OK.

high.name: Evaporated milk
high.notes: Concentrated lactose. Use coconut cream as alternative.

high.name: Custard
high.notes: Contains milk and often wheat. Make with lactose-free milk.

high.name: Baked beans
high.notes: Very high in GOS. Canned and rinsed may be slightly lower.

high.name: Kidney beans
high.notes: High GOS. Canned and well-rinsed reduces content slightly.

high.name: Chickpeas
high.notes: High GOS. Canned (drained/rinsed) in small serves (42g) may be tolerated.

high.name: Lentils
high.notes: High GOS. Canned lentils are lower than dried cooked. Red lentils slightly lower.

high.name: Split peas
high.notes: Very high in GOS. Avoid during elimination phase.

high.name: Soybeans
high.notes: Whole soybeans are high. Firm tofu and tempeh are low FODMAP.

high.name: Honey
high.notes: Very high in excess fructose. Use maple syrup instead.

high.name: Agave syrup
high.notes: Extremely high in fructose. Avoid.

high.name: High-fructose corn syrup
high.notes: Found in soft drinks, sauces, processed foods. Read labels carefully.

high.name: Sorbitol (E420)
high.notes: Used as sweetener in sugar-free gum, mints, diet products.

high.name: Mannitol (E421)
high.notes: Sugar-free sweetener. Found naturally in mushrooms and cauliflower.

high.name: Xylitol (E967)
high.notes: Common in sugar-free gum and dental products.

high.name: Inulin/chicory root
high.notes: Added to many 'high-fiber' products. Check ingredient lists.

low.name: Banana (firm/unripe)
low.notes: Unripe bananas are low FODMAP. Ripe bananas contain more fructose.

low.name: Blueberry
low.notes: Low FODMAP in standard servings (up to 1 cup/150g).

low.name: Strawberry
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 10 medium strawberries.

low.name: Raspberry
low.notes: Low FODMAP in servings up to 60g (about 30 berries).

low.name: Grapes
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to about 1 cup.

low.name: Orange
low.notes: Low FODMAP. One medium orange per sitting.

low.name: Mandarin/clementine
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Limit to 2 small fruits.

low.name: Kiwi
low.notes: Low FODMAP. May also help with constipation.

low.name: Cantaloupe
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 3/4 cup diced.

low.name: Honeydew melon
low.notes: Low FODMAP in servings up to 1/2 cup diced.

low.name: Pineapple
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 1 cup. Contains bromelain which may aid digestion.

low.name: Lemon
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Good for flavoring food.

low.name: Lime
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Use freely as flavoring.

low.name: Passionfruit
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Seeds are edible fiber.

low.name: Carrot
low.notes: Low FODMAP in all servings. Cooked is gentler on the gut.

low.name: Bell pepper (capsicum)
low.notes: Low FODMAP. All colors are suitable.

low.name: Cucumber
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Peeling may reduce fiber load.

low.name: Lettuce (all types)
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Iceberg, butter, rocket all suitable.

low.name: Tomato
low.notes: Low FODMAP fresh. Canned and paste in moderate amounts.

low.name: Spinach
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Excellent source of iron and folate.

low.name: Zucchini (courgette)
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 65g per serve. Good pasta substitute.

low.name: Green beans
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 75g. Steamed or boiled preferred.

low.name: Potato
low.notes: Low FODMAP in all forms. Good energy source during flares.

low.name: Sweet potato
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 1/2 cup (75g). Higher amounts contain mannitol.

low.name: Eggplant (aubergine)
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 1 cup.

low.name: Bok choy
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Good calcium source for dairy-free diets.

low.name: Bean sprouts
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Quick stir-fry ingredient.

low.name: Chives
low.notes: Low FODMAP alternative to onion for mild flavor.

low.name: Ginger
low.notes: Low FODMAP. May help with nausea and inflammation.

low.name: Parsnip
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 1/2 cup. Good roasted.

low.name: Turnip
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Can substitute for higher-FODMAP root vegetables.

low.name: Rice (white, brown, basmati)
low.notes: All rice is low FODMAP. White rice is gentlest during flares.

low.name: Oats
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 1/2 cup dry. Ensure gluten-free if celiac.

low.name: Quinoa
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Complete protein. Good wheat alternative.

low.name: Corn tortilla
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Check labels for added wheat.

low.name: Sourdough spelt bread
low.notes: Fermentation reduces fructan content significantly.

low.name: Gluten-free bread/pasta
low.notes: Usually low FODMAP. Check for high-FODMAP additives like inulin.

low.name: Polenta
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Good side dish alternative.

low.name: Millet
low.notes: Low FODMAP whole grain. Good porridge base.

low.name: Buckwheat
low.notes: Low FODMAP despite the name - not related to wheat.

low.name: Lactose-free milk
low.notes: Lactose removed. Same nutrition as regular milk.

low.name: Hard/aged cheese (cheddar, parmesan, Swiss)
low.notes: Aging removes most lactose. Generally well tolerated.

low.name: Brie/Camembert
low.notes: Low lactose due to aging process.

low.name: Butter
low.notes: Very low lactose. Generally well tolerated.

low.name: Lactose-free yogurt
low.notes: Good probiotic source while avoiding lactose.

low.name: Almond milk (no inulin)
low.notes: Check label for inulin/chicory root fiber which is high FODMAP.

low.name: Chicken
low.notes: All plain meats are low FODMAP. Avoid marinades with garlic/onion.

low.name: Turkey
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Good lean protein source.

low.name: Fish (salmon, tuna, cod)
low.notes: All plain fish are low FODMAP. Omega-3 may help inflammation.

low.name: Eggs
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Versatile protein source.

low.name: Firm tofu
low.notes: Low FODMAP because GOS leaches into water during pressing. Silken tofu is higher.

low.name: Tempeh
low.notes: Fermentation reduces GOS. Good plant protein.

low.name: Beef
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Choose lean cuts. Avoid processed/seasoned versions.

low.name: Pork
low.notes: Plain pork is low FODMAP. Avoid processed sausages with garlic/onion.

low.name: Shrimp/prawns
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Good lean protein.

low.name: Peanuts
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 32 nuts. Peanut butter (2 tbsp) is also low.

low.name: Walnuts
low.notes: Low FODMAP up to 10 halves (30g).

low.name: Maple syrup
low.notes: Low FODMAP sweetener. Good substitute for honey.

low.name: Dark chocolate (>70%)
low.notes: Low FODMAP in small serves (up to 30g). Avoid milk chocolate.

low.name: Olive oil
low.notes: Low FODMAP. Anti-inflammatory. Use freely.

low.name: Soy sauce
low.notes: Low FODMAP in typical servings (2 tbsp).

low.name: Garlic-infused oil
low.notes: FODMAPs are not fat-soluble, so the flavor transfers without the fructans.

low.name: Rice malt syrup
low.notes: Low FODMAP sweetener alternative.
